APPENDIX III.

(Regulations as to Physical Examination. See page 239.)

* For the present sterling equivalents of the salaries specified see page 243.

† These appointments are not set apart exclusively for members of the Police Department, and the Government reserves discretion to fill them by the selection of any officers who are considered best fitted for them, whether in the Department or outside it.

‡ Officers will draw Rs. 250 while they are probationers and Rs. 300 after they are confirmed in the Service as Assistant Superintendents.

INDIAN POLICE FORCE—continued.APPENDIX IV.—LEAVE.

1. The following is a summary of the principal regulations relating to the leave admissible to officers appointed to the Indian Police by the Secretary of State from the United Kingdom.

Long Leave.

2. Furlough is admissible to an aggregate amount of six years during the service. The amount "earned" is one-fourth of an officer's active service, and the amount "due" is that amount less any enjoyed.

3. Furlough without medical certificate can, if due, be generally taken after eight years' active service, and again after three years' continuous service, but is limited to two years at a time.

4. Furlough with medical certificate is limited to one year if an officer has not served three years continuously; otherwise he may have leave up to two years, and it may be extended to a third year.

5. The allowances admissible during furlough are:—

  1. (1) During the first two years of furlough without medical certificate and during so much of furlough with medical certificate as may be "due,"—half average salary, subject to certain maximum and minimum limits.
  2. (2) After the expiration of the period for which the foregoing allowances are admissible—one quarter of average salary, subject to certain maximum and minimum limits.
Short Leave.

6. Privilege Leave is a holiday which may be granted to the extent of one-eleventh part of the time that an officer has been on duty without interruption; and it may be accumulated up to three months, earned by 33 months' service. During absence, the officer retains a lien on his appointment, and receives the salary which he would have received if on duty. An interval of six months must elapse between two periods of absence on privilege leave.

Privilege leave may be prefixed to furlough, special leave, or extraordinary leave without

allowances. The whole period of leave thus taken in combination is known as combined leave. Combined leave may not be of less duration than six months, nor, except on medical certificate, may it be extended beyond two years.

7. Special Leave may be granted at any time for not more than six months, with intervals of six years' service; allowances, calculated as during furlough, are given during the first six months only, whether taken in one or more instalments. This leave is included in the aggregate of six years' furlough admissible.

8. Extraordinary Leave without allowances may be granted in case of necessity, and, except in certain specified cases, only when no other kind of leave is by rule admissible. It may be granted in continuation of other leave.

9. Subsidiary Leave for a minimum of 10 days, usually with half average salary, is granted to an officer proceeding on or returning from leave out of India, or on retirement, to enable him to reach the port of embarkation or to rejoin his appointment. It is admissible only at the end and not at the beginning of combined leave. If the officer is entitled to privilege leave, he can draw pay at privilege leave rates for as many days as he is entitled to such leave.

10. Short Leave is also granted to enable officers to appear at examinations, &c.

General Rules.

11. Leave of absence can never be claimed as of right, and is given or refused at the discretion of Government.

12. After five years' continuous absence from duty, an officer is considered to be out of the employment of Government.

13. When leave allowances are paid at the Home Treasury, or in a Colony where the standard of currency is gold, rupees are converted into sterling at the rate of exchange fixed for the time being for the adjustment of financial transactions between the Imperial and Indian Treasuries, unless any other rate has been exceptionally authorised. But for the present the rate of conversion is subject to a minimum of 1s. 6d. to the rupee.

APPENDIX V.—PENSIONS AND PROVIDENT FUND.

1. The following is a summary of the pension rules applicable to officers of the Indian Police appointed by the Secretary of State from the United Kingdom.

An officer of the Indian Police becomes eligible for a pension on completing 30 years' qualifying service, or on attaining the age of 55 years. If at an earlier date he is compelled to retire from the service through ill-health, not occasioned by irregular or intemperate habits, he becomes eligible for an invalid pension or a gratuity according to the length of his service.

The amount of pension or gratuity is regulated as follows:—

After a service of less than 10 years.—A gratuity not exceeding one month's emoluments for each completed year of service.

After a service of not less than 10 years.—A pension not exceeding the following amounts:—

Years of Completed Service. Sixtieths of Average Emoluments. Maximum Limit of Pension.
10 10 Rs. 2,000 a year.
11 11 2,200 "
12 12 2,400 "
13 13 2,600 "
14 14 2,800 "
INDIAN POLICE FORCE—continued.
Years of Completed Service. Sixtieths of Average Emoluments. Maximum Limit of Pension.
15 15 Rs. 3,000 a year.
16 16 3,200 "
17 17 3,400 "
18 18 3,600 "
19 19 3,800 "
20 20 4,000 "
21 21 4,200 "
22 22 4,400 "
23 23 4,600 "
24 24 4,800 "
25 and above 30 5,000 "

2. A Provident Fund has also been established on the following basis:—

  1. (1) The contribution is compulsory up to 5 per cent. on salaries, with voluntary contributions of a further 5 per cent.
  2. (2) Compound interest on such payments is annually credited by Government to each officer subscribing, the rate being at present 4 per cent. per annum.
  3. (3) Receipts and payments are made in rupees only.
  4. (4) The sum which will thus accumulate to the credit of an officer to be his absolute property to be handed over to him, unconditionally, on quitting the service; or in the event of his death before retirement, to his legal representatives.
ROYAL INDIAN ENGINEERING COLLEGE, COOPER'S HILL.

The Secretary of State reserves to himself the right to revise, modify, or change any of the provisions herein contained from time to time, as he may see fit.

Notice.—In view of the decision which has been arrived at by the Secretary of State for India in Council, the Royal Indian Engineering College will be permanently closed at the end of the session of 1905-6.

The Entrance Examination held in 1904 will be the last, and the College course for the successful Candidates at that Examination will extend to two years (six terms) only instead of to three years (nine terms) as heretofore.

Extra Students.—When there is room for more students than are under training for the public service, it is within the discretion of the President, subject to the orders of the Secretary of State, to admit others who are desirous of obtaining the education given at the College.

Such extra students are not eligible for prizes, scholarships or appointments; but in other respects they shall have the same privileges and be subject to the same discipline and rules as the other students.

Annual Session.—The College Year begins in September, and is divided into three terms, with vacations of about four weeks at Christmas, three weeks at Easter, and eight weeks in summer. The summer term in 1906 will, however, for Engineering Students who entered in 1904, be extended into October, with a view to preparation for the Examination of the Institution of Civil Engineers in that month. For this special course the fee for one term, £61, will be charged.

The Fees and Charges.—An annual charge of £183 is made for each student, which must be paid in advance to the Bank of England in three sums of £61 per term. Receivable Orders with full directions as to the mode of payment will be forwarded from the India Office to the Parents or Guardians shortly before the fees fall due. A student will not be admitted into residence until his fee has been paid. The voluntary subscriptions to the "General Recreation Fund" are covered by an additional payment of £6 per annum, which, for convenience,

will be collected in six instalments of £1 each, falling due in the first two months of each term.

A deposit of £5 is required from each student on admission to the College, as caution money, to cover charges incurred by him for damage to books, instruments, &c., or any College bills outstanding on leaving the College. When these have been defrayed the balance standing at his credit will be repaid. This deposit is to be paid with the fee for the first term, making the total payment on that occasion £66.

The College fees include all charges for tuition, board according to the College scale, lodging, ordinary medical attendance, and washing to the extent of 2s. weekly. Students are required to provide their own class books and drawing instruments. Drawing paper, drawing boards, and surveying instruments are provided by the College.

Refund of Fees.—In the case of a student leaving the College, from whatever cause, after the commencement of a term, no refund of the fees paid in advance can be claimed as a right. If a student so leaving has not resided longer than 30 days since the commencement of the term for which payment in advance has been made, a refund may be made, as a matter of grace, of such sum as the Secretary of State may approve in each case.

Accommodation Provided.—Each student is provided with a separate room, and with fuel and light, also with the necessary attendance. Furniture and bedding are supplied by the College, but each student is required to provide his own towels and bed linen. Meals are taken in Hall. Wine and beer are not included in the ordinary fare, but can be obtained from the College cellar at fixed prices.

Subjects of Study.—The subjects of study at the College are:—

  • Pure and Applied Mathematics.
  • Geometrical, Engineering, and Freehand Drawing.
  • Building Construction.
ROYAL INDIAN ENGINEERING COLLEGE, COOPER'S HILL—continued.

Estimating.

Surveying.

Engineering Construction, including the materials used, the theory and practice of using the same to the best advantage in various classes of engineering works, and the theory and principles of construction of machines used in engineering works.

Workshop Practice.

Electrical Engineering.

Chemistry; to the extent required to enable the Engineer to interpret results given by professional chemists.

Physics: heat, light and sound, electricity and magnetism.

Geology, the elements of.

French:—Optional for { Obligatory for
Engineer Students. { Telegraph Students.

Telegraph Construction } For Telegraph
Telegraph Signalling } Students only.

Means of Instruction.—Chemical, physical, electro-technical, botanical, mechanical and hydraulic laboratories, a library, gymnasium, carpenter's shop and workshops provided with steam, oil, and gas engines, machinery, and electric light, are attached to the College. Means are also provided for the practice of photography. Students making use of the laboratories are supplied with the needful apparatus. They are charged for the use of the tools provided in the carpenter's shop, and are required to provide their own hand-tools and material in the other workshops.*

Minimum Qualification for Diploma.—The subjects enumerated above are grouped in certain main branches of study, and a fixed minimum of qualification is required in each branch, as well as a certain minimum in each obligatory subject, in order to obtain the ordinary College diploma. Superior attainments are recognised by special diplomas.

Examinations.—(a) Periodical.—The proficiency of the students in the studies pursued is tested by periodical examinations, and by assigning values to the drawings, surveys, notes, reports, &c., executed by them while at the College.

(b) Final.—A final examination is held during the last year of the course, with the assistance of special examiners not connected with the College. This final examination, in addition to paper work, includes practical exercises in surveying, drawing, designing, and estimating.

Diplomas and Honours.—Those students who pass out of the College with very special distinction are appointed "Honorary Fellows of Cooper's Hill." The Diploma of "Associate" is granted to all others who pass out in the First Class in one at least of the branches of study. All others who come up to the prescribed standard of qualification receive the ordinary diploma of the College.

APPOINTMENTS IN THE PUBLIC SERVICE OF INDIA.

General.—It is hereby declared that the Secretary of State for India, in making his selection of persons to be appointed from the College to the public service in India, does not

bind himself to be guided wholly by the order of merit list. He will select such persons as he thinks in all respects best qualified for that branch of the service for which they may be required, and will have regard to the moral character, habits of discipline, soundness of constitution and physique of the candidates, as well as to their proficiency in their studies.

Appointments.

The appointments in the gift of the Secretary of State open to students of the Royal Indian Engineering College are:—

  1. (1) In the Engineering Branch of the Public Works Department.
  2. (2) In the Accounts Branch of the same.
  3. (3) In the Telegraph Department.
Engineering Branch, Public Works Department.

Selection.—The selection for the Engineering Branch will be made from those students who shall have completed the three years' course, passed the final examination, and obtained the College diploma.

Candidates for the Engineering Branch who entered in 1904 will be required to complete the course of study at Cooper's Hill commenced in September, 1904.

For these candidates an examination will be held in July, 1906, after which 25 candidates will be provisionally selected for appointment. In making this selection the Secretary of State does not bind himself to be guided wholly by the order of merit as resulting from the examination.

The candidates so selected will further be required:—

  1. (1) To remain in the College for a special course of instruction until October, 1906. (A fee of £61 will be charged for this special course, lasting from July to October.)
  2. (2) To pass the examination held in October, 1906 by the Institution of Civil Engineers for Associate Membership or, in the case of any student who is then too young to enter, the first subsequent Examination held by the Institution after the candidate is old enough to enter for the same.
  3. (3) To undergo, if required, after leaving the College, a course of practical training on works to be selected by the Secretary of State.

Initial Appointments and Salary.—On selection students will be appointed Assistant Engineers, third grade, on a salary of Rs. 4,200 †

* The cost of the hand-tools purchased, as well as the charge for the use of others, and for materials used in the ordinary course, may be taken at about £3 10s. If a student desires to make any special apparatus, and can be permitted to do so without interfering with the course, he will be charged with the cost of the materials.

† For the present sterling equivalent of this salary see page 243.

ROYAL INDIAN ENGINEERING COLLEGE, COOPER'S HILL—continued.

per annum, which will commence from the date of their landing in India.

Posting and Liability to be Transferred.—In posting Assistant Engineers to the different provinces and branches of work, the Secretary of State will be guided by the interests of the public service; but he will have regard so far as may be to the wishes of the selected students. It must be understood, however, that all officers are liable to be transferred by order of the Government of India, according to the exigencies of the service, from one province to another, or from one sort of work to another within the limits of the service for which they have been selected.

Practical Course.—In each year a few Assistant Engineers, to be selected by the President, may be detained in England for the purpose of undergoing a practical course in some special branch of their profession.

Duration of Course and Salary.—The duration of this course will be specially fixed in each individual case, but will not ordinarily exceed one year. While employed on it, the Assistant Engineer will receive pay at the rate of £150 a year, to commence from the date of entering on the practical course, and in addition will be eligible to receive a premium of from £10 to £25 in each half year during the course, according to the degree of proficiency and diligence shown.

Fees Payable for Course.—The fees to the Engineers or others under whom the practical course is passed will be paid by the Secretary of State for India; but if the Assistant Engineer voluntarily relinquishes his appointment, they will have to be refunded.

Departure for India.—Assistant Engineers will be required to embark for India ordinarily within three months after their appointment, or, if they have been detained for the practical course, one month after the completion of the course. They will be granted free passages to India, and their salaries will commence from the date of their landing in India.

Service for Pension.—Service for pension will count from the 1st day of October of the year of their appointment.

Accounts Branch, Public Works Department.

Selection.—The selection for the Accounts Branch will be made from those students who shall have completed a two years' course at the College, and shall be desirous of obtaining appointments in that branch.

Initial Appointment and Salary.—The selected students will be appointed to be Assistant Examiners, first grade, on a salary of Rs. 3,600 per annum.*

Embarkation and Passage.—They will be required to embark for India within three months of their being appointed. They will be provided with free passages, and their salaries will commence from the date of their landing in India.

Service for Pension.—Their service for pension will count from the 1st day of October in the year of their appointment.

The Telegraph Department.

Selection.—The selection for the Telegraph Department will be made from those students who shall have completed one year of their College course, and shall be candidates for employment in that Department.

Qualifications.—In making the selection weight will be given to proficiency in mathematics.

Special Training.—Students selected will be considered probationers in the Telegraph Department, and the remaining two years of their College course will be devoted to special training for the service.

The candidates selected at the Entrance Examination of 1904 for this Department will be required to complete a course of study at Cooper's Hill, commencing in September, 1904, and ending in July, 1906, and to pass satisfactory examinations during and at the end of that course.

Initial Appointment and Salary.—On final appointment candidates will enter the service as Assistant Superintendents, 4th grade, of the Telegraph Department, and will receive an initial salary of Rs. 4,200 per annum.*

Embarkation and Passage.—The Assistant Superintendents will be required to embark for India within three months after their appointment. They will be provided with free passages to India, and their salaries will commence from the date of their landing in India.

Service for Pension.—Their service for pension will count from the 1st day of October in the year of their appointment.

General.

Medical Examination.—Candidates selected for appointment to the Public Service will be required to pass an examination held by the Medical Board at the India Office as to physical fitness for service in India.

Riding.—Selected candidates for the Public Works (Engineering Branch) and Telegraph Departments must be able to ride, and their appointments are made subject to their producing satisfactory evidence that they possess this qualification.

Future Promotion.—In the case of both branches of the Public Works Department and of the Telegraph Department, promotion after arrival in India will rest with the Indian Government, and will be subject to such conditions, as to passing Departmental Examinations in vernacular languages and other subjects, as that Government may from time to time direct.

* For the present sterling equivalent of the salary specified see page 213.

ROYAL INDIAN ENGINEERING COLLEGE, COOPER'S HILL—continued.

Indian Appointments for Students who entered the College in 1904.—(1) In the Engineering Branch of the Public Works Department, 25 appointments; (2) in the Accounts Branch of the same, 1 appointment; (3) in the Telegraph Department, 2 appointments.

Students who fail to obtain Government Service.—The President is prepared, by

endeavouring to find employment for them elsewhere, to do what he can to help students who have obtained the diploma, but have not been selected for the Government Service. For such extra appointments as may be placed at the disposal of the President, the passing of the Associateship Examination of the Institution of Civil Engineers will be accorded due weight.

INDIAN PUBLIC WORKS DEPARTMENT.INFORMATION AS TO APPOINTMENTS OF ASSISTANT ENGINEERS, 1905.

1. The Secretary of State for India in Council will, in the summer of 1905, make not less than eleven appointments of Assistant Engineers to the Public Works Department of the Government of India, in addition to those to be made from the Royal Indian Engineering College at Cooper's Hill.

In making these appointments he will act with the advice of a Selection Committee, including at least one eminent representative of the engineering profession.

2. Applications for the appointments must be made on a printed form to be obtained from the Secretary, Judicial and Public Department, India Office, Whitehall, London, S.W., and to be returned to him not earlier than 1st March, 1905, or later than Monday, the 1st May, 1905.

No application received after the latter date will be considered.

3. The age of Candidates must be not less than 21, or more than 24, years on the 1st July, 1905.

4. Every Candidate must be a British subject of European descent, and at the time of his birth his father must have been a British subject, either natural-born or naturalised in the United Kingdom. The decision of the Secretary of State in Council as to whether a Candidate satisfies this condition shall be final. He must also be of good moral character and sound physique.

5. Candidates must have obtained one of the University degrees mentioned below, or such diploma or other distinction in Engineering at some Institution connected with one of the Universities named as may, in the opinion of the Vice-Chancellor of that University, be approximately equivalent thereto.

6. They must further have had at least one year's experience as assistant in the preparation of the designs for, or in the execution of, some engineering work of importance.

The Secretary of State may, however, if he thinks fit, relax this rule so as to allow the period of one year to be completed before the departure of a Candidate for India in October, 1905. (See paragraph 10 below.)

7. Candidates must be prepared, if called upon, to attend at the India Office, at their own expense, for personal interview with the Selection Committee.

8. They will further be required before final appointment by the Secretary of State in Council to appear before the Medical Board at the India Office for examination as to their physical fitness for service in India.

A copy of the "Regulations as to the Physical Examination" of Candidates for Indian appointments will be forwarded on application to the Under Secretary of State. (See page 239.)

9. Candidates will also be required, before final appointment, to satisfy the Secretary of State, in such manner as he may determine, of their ability to ride.

10. They will on appointment be provided with free first class passages to India, and they will be expected to proceed thither in the month of October, 1905.

Their pay will begin from the date of their landing in India.

11. They will enter the Service as Assistant Engineers, Third Grade, on a salary of Rs. 4,200 per annum, and they will receive the same salary and privileges as the Assistant Engineers passing out of the Royal Indian Engineering College at Cooper's Hill in the same year.

They will be graded in the Department immediately below these last.

12. Particulars as to their prospects of pay, pension, &c., will be found on the next page.

India Office,
November, 1904.

LIST OF DEGREES REFERRED TO IN PARA. 5.

University of London.—B.Sc. (Engineering).

University of Cambridge.—B.A. (Mechanical Science Tripos).

University of St. Andrews.—B.Sc. (Engineering).

University of Glasgow.—B.Sc. (Engineering).

University of Edinburgh.—B.Sc. (Engineering).

University of Dublin.—B.A.I.

Royal University of Ireland.—B.E. and M.E.

University of Wales.—B.Sc. (Engineering), provided that Mathematics be passed at the final examination for the degree.

The late Victoria University.—B.Sc. (with honours in Engineering).

Any other degree of a University in the United Kingdom which may hereafter be recognised by the Council of the Institution of Civil Engineers as exempting from passing the examination for Associate Membership.

PUBLIC WORKS DEPARTMENT—EXECUTIVE BRANCH.

[The arrangements and salaries hereinafter described are subject to revision according to the requirements of the service.]

1. The Engineer Establishment of the Indian Public Works Department consists of the staff of engineers, military and civil, engaged on the construction and maintenance of the various public works undertaken by the State in India.

2. The permanent establishment of the Department is recruited from the following sources:—

  1. (1.) Officers of Royal Engineers.
  2. (2.) Officers of the Indian Army who have passed the qualifying examination.
  3. (3.) Persons appointed to the Imperial Service by the Secretary of State by selection from the United Kingdom.
  4. (4.) Persons educated at the Government Civil Engineering Colleges in India, and appointed to the Provincial Services by the Government of India.
  5. (5.) Occasional admission of other qualified persons.

3. The various ranks of the department are as follows:—

Salary per annum.*
Rs.
Chief Engineers, First Class ... 30,000
" " Second Class ... 24,000
" " Third Class ... 21,600
Superintending Engineer, First Class ... 19,200
" " Second Class ... 16,800
" " Third Class ... 15,000
Executive Engineers, First Grade 12,000
" " Second Grade 10,200
" " Third Grade 8,400
Assistant Engineers, First Grade 6,600
" " Second Grade 5,400
" " Third Grade 4,200

4. Officers who are finally selected from the United Kingdom for appointment to the Indian Public Works Department will, in the absence of any special reasons to the contrary, be appointed to the rank of Assistant Engineers, Third Grade.

5. Promotions from one grade or class to another are dependent on the occurrence of vacancies in the sanctioned establishment, and are regulated in the following way.

6. Promotions throughout all grades in Madras and Bombay are made by the Governments of these provinces respectively; also in Bengal, the United Provinces of Agra and Oudh, the Punjab, and Burma, except as regards the appointments of Chief and Superintending Engineer, which are made by the Government of India.

7. In the other provinces,† promotions are made by the Government of India, on one general list for all these provinces.

8. Promotions in the Railway Branch of the Department are made by the Government of India.

9. Promotion is made wholly by selection; mere seniority is considered to confer no claim to it.

PENSIONS AND PROVIDENT FUND.

10. The scale of Pensions below applies to Engineers appointed to the Imperial Service by the Secretary of State from the United Kingdom.

11. A Provident Fund has been established for all Civil Engineers of the Department on the following basis:—

  1. (1.) The contribution is compulsory up to 5 per cent. on salaries, with voluntary contributions of a further 5 per cent.
  2. (2.) Compound interest on such payments is annually credited by Government to each officer subscribing, the rate being at present 4 per cent. per annum in the case of accounts kept on a rupee basis, and 3 per cent. per annum in the case of those kept on a sterling basis. Members of the Fund are given the option of having their accounts kept either on a rupee or on a sterling basis.
  3. (3.) The sum which will thus accumulate to the credit of an officer to be his absolute property, to be handed over to him, unconditionally, on quitting the service; or, in the event of his death before retirement, to his legal representatives.
SCALE OF PENSIONS.
No. of Years' Service. Sixtieth Parts of Average Emoluments.‡ Subject to a Maximum of Rupees per annum. No. of Years' Service. Sixtieth Parts of Average Emoluments.‡ Subject to a Maximum of Rupees per annum.
ON MEDICAL CERTIFICATE.

11 21 1,400 16 26

14 24 2,600 19 29

† Central Provinces (including Berar), Coorg, Assam, Rajputana and Central India, and Baluchistan.

‡ "Average emoluments" means the average calculated on the last three years of service.

PUBLIC WORKS DEPARTMENT—EXECUTIVE BRANCH—continued.
