SELECTIONS FROM THE INDIAN MILITARY REGULATIONS.

The following regulations are published for information, but it should be understood that they are only selections. Complete copies of the Indian Army Regulations can be seen on personal application to the Military Department, India Office.

INDIAN ARMY.

Examination for Advancement.*

Officers of the Indian Army are not qualified to hold the appointments of Commandant,† Second in Command, or Squadron or Double Company Commander, and are ineligible for appointment to the Staff of the Army (unless they come within the provisions of the following paragraphs), until they shall have passed an examination in the following subjects:—

PART I.

  1. 1. Handling a regiment in the field.
  2. 2. Riding (Infantry officers).

PART II.

  1. 3. (c.) Duties in the field (practical and oral).
    1. (i) Practical military topography.
    2. (ii) Practical military engineering.
    3. (iii) Practical tactics.
    4. (iv) Horsemanship (for mounted branches only).
    5. (v) Riding.

(d.) Theoretical and paper examination.

  1. (i) Military engineering, tactics, and topography.
  2. (ii) Military law.
  3. (iii) Administration, organisation, and equipment.
  4. (iv) Military history.

4. Indian military law.

A graduate of the Staff College will be examined in (1) and (4) only. An officer who in the rank of Captain has qualified for admission to the Staff College will be examined in (1), (2), and (4).

A certificate of having passed through a course of instruction in fortification at the School of Military Engineering will be accepted in lieu of examination in (c.) (ii).

A certificate of having performed the duties of permanent Adjutant for 12 months continuously will be accepted in lieu of (1).

Promotion.

Under Royal Warrant promotion in the Indian Army is governed by such Regulations as the Secretary of State for India may make from time to time.

The existing regulations for promotion, up to the rank of Lieutenant-Colonel, are—

I. Except as otherwise provided, promotion is regulated by length of service from date of first permanent commission.

Officers of 2 years 3 months' service become Lieutenants;

After 9 years' service they become Captains;

After 18 years' service, they become Majors;

and

After 26 years' service, they become Lieutenant-Colonels.

II. No officer is promoted unless he is pronounced fit and is recommended for promotion.

An officer whose promotion has been retarded under this rule is not entitled to further promotion until he has served nine years in the rank of Captain and eight years in the rank of Major.

III. A Second Lieutenant is not promoted to the rank of Lieutenant until he has passed the examination in Urdu by the lower standard.

IV. A Lieutenant of not less than six years' service from date of first permanent commission may be promoted to the rank of Captain if he holds or is appointed to the command of a Squadron or Double Company.

The number of promotions under this rule is limited to five in each year.

V. A Captain may be promoted to the rank of Major if he holds or is appointed to one of the following posts:—

* The qualifications required and Examinations to be passed for the Judge Advocate-General's Department, the Administrative Departments of the Army, the Survey Department, and for Civil and Political Employ, will be found in the Indian Army Regulations.

† Officers will not be appointed to the command of regiments until they have passed the prescribed examination in "Tactical Fitness for Command."

INDIAN ARMY—continued.

  • (a) Second in command of a regiment or battalion;
  • (b) Deputy Assistant Adjutant-General or Deputy Assistant Quartermaster-General;
  • (c) Supply and Transport Officer, Second or Third class.

The number of promotions under this rule is limited to five in each year.

VI. A Major may be promoted to the rank of Lieutenant-Colonel on appointment to one of the following posts:—

  • (a) Command of a regiment or battalion;
  • (b) An appointment which would carry the substantive rank of Colonel if the officer were qualified for that rank;
  • (c) Supply and Transport Officer, First class;
  • (d) Controller of Military Accounts;

subject as regards (c) to the officer only being promoted if specially selected for promotion.

VII. An officer specially promoted under Rules IV. and V. is eligible, subject to Rule II., for further promotion after not more than nine years' service in the rank of Captain, and eight years' service in the rank of Major.

Time on half pay not exceeding one year is allowed to reckon as service for promotion in the case of an officer placed on half pay on account of ill-health contracted in the performance of military duty, but no officer can be promoted while actually on the half-pay list.

An officer holding the rank of Lieutenant-Colonel may be promoted to the brevet rank of Colonel—

  • (a) On appointment as Aide-de-Camp to the Sovereign.
  • (b) On appointment to a position specified in the following paragraph, if, at the date of his appointment, he holds regimental rank not above that of Major and has completed three years' full-pay service as brevet Lieutenant-Colonel.
  • (c) On completion of three years' service in

command of a regiment of Cavalry or battalion of Infantry, or in any appointment tenable by a Lieutenant-Colonel which may be considered to be analogous thereto. One-half of any previous full-pay service as substantive or brevet Lieutenant-Colonel may be reckoned in reduction of this period.

  • (d) On completion of six years' full-pay service as substantive or brevet Lieutenant-Colonel in a military appointment.

A brevet Colonel, or a Lieutenant-Colonel having three years' full-pay service in that rank (except a Lieutenant-Colonel who receives on his appointment the brevet rank of Colonel under (b) above), may be granted the substantive rank of Colonel, if selected for or holding certain appointments approved by the Secretary of State in Council as carrying the substantive rank of Colonel.

Officers above the rank of Lieutenant are also eligible for promotion by brevet as a reward for distinguished service in the field, or for distinguished service of an exceptional nature other than in the field.

Promotion to the ranks of Major-General and Lieutenant-General are only made by selection to fill appointments, or as a reward for distinguished service. There is an establishment of three Generals, but no Officer may be promoted to General by seniority until he has served three years in the rank of Lieutenant-General.

Officers in civil employ, after ten years' absence from military duty, are removed from the effective list of the army, and placed on a supernumerary list, rising thereon, under the regulations in force, to the rank of Lieutenant-Colonel, but to no higher rank.

This condition is, however, not applicable to officers of rank taken for high political or civil employment, or to officers taken for temporary civil or political duties in the field or in newly-acquired territories.

Officers below the rank of Major-General on leave out of India receive leave pay at the following rates:—

Half-Pay.

Yearly. Yearly.
£ £
Field Marshal ... .. 1,300 Lieutenant-General ... .. 650
General ... .. 800 Major-General ... .. 500

Half-pay is only granted to other officers on account of certified unfitness for duty (see page 333).

The rates of half-pay are those in force in the British Service, namely:—

Daily. Daily.
s. d. s. d.
Lieutenant-Colonel ... .. 11 0 Lieutenant (after six years' commissioned
Major ... .. 9 6 service) ... .. 4 0
Captain ... .. 7 0 " (after ten years' commissioned
" having higher rank by brevet ... .. 8 0 service) ... .. 4 6
Lieutenant ... .. 3 0

An officer cannot remain more than six years on temporary half-pay.

LEAVE RULES of 1886 FOR THE INDIAN ARMY.

(India Army Circulars, April, 1886, Clause 51, published 16th April, with subsequent decisions.)

I. An officer may be granted,—

Privilege leave under such regulations as may from time to time be in force.

Leave out of India, for no longer period than one year, capable of extension to two years' absence from duty, on the following pay:—

After appointment to the Indian Army ... .. 200 a year.

After the commencement of the 5th year's service for pension ... .. 250 "

Do. do., 15th, do., do., do. 300 "

Do. do., 20th, do., do., do. 450 "

Do. do., 25th, do., do., do. 600 "

Do. do., 30th, do., do., do. 700 "

Leave in India, but for the period of one year only, on full military pay, and half the Staff salary of appointment.

II. No extension of leave involving absence from duty for more than two years, whether taken in or out of India, can be granted except on specially urgent grounds and without pay.

III. An officer unable on account of the state of his health to return to duty within the maximum period of two years' absence, will, unless he is under rule II. specially granted an extension of leave without pay, be placed on temporary half pay or the retired list, as the circumstances of the case may seem to require. An officer will also be liable to be placed on half pay or the retired list should his health require an undue amount of leave, whether in or out of India. This power will be exercised by

the Government of India, at the instance of the authority empowered to sanction the leave, should the officer be in India, or, should he be at home, by the Secretary of State in Council.

IV. All leave, in or out of India, counts as service for pension.

V. Leave, whether it is leave in India or leave out of India, will not involve forfeiture of appointment, and may be granted at any time, but solely at the discretion of the sanctioning authority. In the case of officers temporarily serving under the Lieutenant-General of a command other than their own, application for leave should be forwarded for final sanction through the Lieutenant-General of the command in which they are serving to the Lieutenant-General of the command to which they belong, except in urgent cases, when the authority to grant leave will rest with the former, a report of the grant in every case being at once made to the Deputy Adjutant-General of the command to which the officer belongs.

VI. An officer on leave, whether in India or out of India, will be required to rejoin at once on being recalled to duty, unless certified by a Medical Board as unfit to do so.

VII. The above rules shall apply to—

Officers whose admission to the Indian Army bears date subsequent to the 30th June, 1886.

Officers of the Indian Army, including probationers, in military employment, who may elect them within twelve months of that date.

INDIAN ARMY—continued.LEAVE RULES OF 1886 FOR THE INDIAN ARMY—continued.

Officers in civil employ on that date, subsequently reverting to military duty, may elect them within six months from the date of being replaced at the disposal of the Military Department. Such officers who may be on leave when so replaced must make their election at once. Those taking leave within the six months must make their election before going on leave.

They shall not apply to—

  • Officers in permanent civil employ.
  • Officers commanding districts or holding other appointments under special regulations as to leave of absence, who continue subject to such regulations.

Note.—The above does not preclude general officers commanding districts, who before their appointment were subject to the leave rules of 1886 for the Indian Army, from being granted short leave in India under these rules.

VIII. Officers who, have elected to come under these rules count all previous leave as service for pension. The position of those who have not elected them remains unchanged.

IX. The Secretary of State in Council reserves the right of making any change in the foregoing regulations which circumstances may require.

Officers who have not elected for the above rules in the manner and within the period laid down in Rule VII have no further opportunity of election, but remain under previous rules. It must also be clearly understood that no modifications of the existing codes will be sanctioned, and that there is no middle course between abiding by them and acceptance of these new rules.

Military officers in civil employ on 30th June, 1886, who may desire to retire direct from such employment, have, on such retirement, the option of electing the new rules.

When it may be necessary to convert sterling rates of pay under these rules into rupee currency, or vice versa, the conversion will be made at the rate of exchange in force for the period when the pay became due, as fixed annually for the adjustment of financial transactions between the Indian and Imperial Governments.

The rules which govern the allowances of officers acting for absentees remain unchanged.

INDIAN MEDICAL SERVICE.

The rules laid down for the grant of leave to officers of the Indian Army apply equally to officers of the Indian Medical Service in military employ, with the following modification in regard to the rates of pay whilst on leave out of India:—

After arrival in India £
After the commencement of the 10th year's service for pension ... 250 a year.
Ditto, ditto, 15th ditto ... 450 "
Ditto, ditto, 20th ditto ... 600 "
Ditto, ditto, 25th ditto ... 700 "

The rules apply to all Medical Officers admitted to the service after the 30th June, 1886, and to officers who may elect them under the provisions of Rule VII.

SUBSIDIARY RULES.

1. Officers in permanent civil employment, while they continue in such employment, are not affected by these rules; but if desirous of retiring on pension direct from such employment, they will, whether on duty or on furlough, be given the benefit of the rule which allows all previous leave to count as service for pension.

It is, however, to be clearly understood that no officer in civil employment will be allowed to place himself out of employment for the purpose of taking leave under these rules, or for any purpose but immediate retirement.

2. Leave in or out of India commences on the date an officer is "struck off duty," which means the date following the day of relief, and terminates on, and includes, the day preceding the date on which he rejoins his regiment or appointment on return from leave.

3. In the case of an officer obliged to leave his station to appear before a Medical Board at another station, the date of being "struck off duty" will mean the date he is permitted to quit the station where he is examined and invalided. An officer so circumstanced is entitled to full pay and Staff salary up to and for the date preceding that on which he is permitted to quit the station where he is examined and invalided. In the case of an officer on the sick list, while actually present with his corps, or at his station, the date of being "struck off duty" means the date on which he leaves his corps or station.

4. Under these rules, absence from duty with pay, which is restricted to two years, is reckoned from the date of being "struck off duty" to date of rejoining appointment or regiment.

5. Up to date of leaving India, and from date of return to India to date of rejoining regiment or appointment, an officer granted leave out of India under these rules will receive Indian pay and half staff salary, provided, in any case, the two years' absence from duty is not exceeded.

6. An officer subject to these rules who holds a permanent departmental or regimental staff appointment, promoted while on leave out of India, is only entitled, from date of debarkation in India to that of joining his appointment, to the Indian pay and allowances of his rank, and the half staff salary of the appointment he held at the time he proceeded on leave.

7. An officer holding a permanent departmental or regimental staff appointment, promoted while on leave in India, will draw the Indian pay and allowances of his rank, and the half staff salary of the appointment he held at the time he proceeded on leave, until he assumes charge of the duties of the appointment to which he has been promoted.

8. The Indian Pension Regulations are unaffected by these leave rules, except that all leave from date of commencement of service for pension will, under them, be allowed to count as service for pension.

9. Time passed at Addiscombe after the attainment of the age of 16 years will reckon as service for pension in calculating the rates of pay under Rule I.

INDIAN ARMY—continued.LEAVE RULES OF 1886 FOR THE INDIAN ARMY—continued.

10. The periods of service for pension in Rule I of the leave rules entitle an officer taking leave to the pay fixed for such periods of service, without any limitation based on the rule* which prevents an officer from retiring on the pension fixed for a particular period of service, unless he has served half of that service in the Indian Army.

11. The provisions of the rules with regard to duration of leave do not apply to probationers for the Indian Army.

12. The limit of two years laid down for leave will not apply to time spent at the Staff College, which counts as duty.

13. Officers recalled from leave granted under these rules will be given free passage to India, or not, according to the Transport Regulations in force at the time.

14. Advances of pay to officers proceeding on and returning from leave out of India will be granted at the rates shown on pages 339 and 340.

15. Extensions of leave, except on medical certificate or in special or urgent cases in which

the Secretary of State decides according to circumstances, cannot be granted without the assent of the proper authority in India as defined in Clause V of the rules.

16. Extensions of leave beyond one year under these rules are not claimable in any way as a right, but are admissible only at the discretion of the proper authority, and then on special and urgent grounds.

17. An officer subject to these rules on promotion to Major-General, or on attainment of Colonel's allowance, cannot be granted leave under them, and, if permitted to reside in England or elsewhere out of India, is restricted to the English pay of his rank.

18. Officers subject to these rules, who are in the following categories, and who entered the Indian Army before the 1st July, 1881, may elect to reside out of India on the allowances admissible under them until succession to the Colonel's allowance or promotion to Major-General, whichever may happen first; or to remain in India on the unemployed Indian pay and allowances of their rank if unemployed:—

G. G. O. 209 of 1882, para. 7. (a) Officers vacating regimental commands, remaining in the service for the attainment of the highest retirement pension, or the Colonel's allowance.

(b) Officers over 55 years of age, who may be required under the rules to vacate staff, departmental, or civil appointments, and for whom other suitable employment cannot be found.

G. G. O. 192 of 1883. (c) Officers of the Commissariat-Transport, Military Accounts, and Judge Advocate General's Department, who vacate their appointments on attaining to 52 years of age.

G. G. O. 432 of 1884, Clause IV (iii). (d) Officers who on the 8th August, 1884, were holding appointments of unlimited tenure to which they were appointed before the 1st January, 1884, and who vacate their appointments on completing seven years' tenure from date of appointment or such further extension as may be granted to enable them to complete 32 years' service for pension not exceeding nine years in all.

G. G. O. 310 of 1885. (e) Squadron or double-company commanders who vacate their appointments on attaining 52 years of age.

G. G. O. 432 of 1884, Clause VII. (f) Colonels who entered the Indian Army after the 12th September, 1866, and before the 1st July, 1881, and Colonels of the General List, after transfer to the Unemployed Supernumerary List.

The limit of absence from duty imposed by these leave rules is not applicable to these officers.

19. Officers who may have entered civil employment after the 30th June, 1886, and before the 1st July, 1887, without having elected these rules, will be entitled, on reverting to military duty, to elect them under the conditions laid down in the third clause of Rule VII. (See note to next paragraph.)

20. Officers who may have entered civil employment after the 30th June, 1886, having previously elected these rules, and officers who may have entered the Indian Army under them and subsequently taken civil employment, are absolutely under the leave rules contained in the Civil Service Regulations so long as they remain in permanent civil employment, but on reverting to military duty within ten years of their entry into civil employment, as allowed under G. G. O. No. 135 of 1884, they again come under these rules.

Note.—An officer does not come under civil rules by reason of being transferred to a civil appointment, the tenure of which is limited to a definite period, nor by officiating for less than three years in the civil department.

21. Officers struck off the effective list of the army under the provisions of G. G. O. No. 135 of 1884, after ten years' absence from military duty, cannot elect or revert to these rules except for the purpose of immediate retirement, with the advantage of counting all leave as service for pension, as provided for in paragraph 1.

22. The service for furlough of an officer, who at the time of coming under the civil leave rules, was subject to the leave rules of 1886 for the Indian Army, is calculated in accordance with the civil leave rules retrospectively from the date of his arrival in India, i.e., he is credited with an amount of furlough equivalent to one-

INDIAN ARMY—continued.LEAVE RULES OF 1886 FOR THE INDIAN ARMY—continued.

fourth of his active service, less any leave out of India actually taken.

It is notified that all officers in military employ, irrespective of rank or the furlough or leave regulations to which they may be subject, who require an extension on private affairs of their furlough or leave out of India, must obtain the previous assent of the authorities in India under

whom they may be serving, to apply for such extension.

This permission, when obtained, must be attached to their application to the India Office for the required extension.

Note.—This memorandum has been compiled for easy reference, but it is not intended for quotation as of official authority.

COMBINED LEAVE.

(INDIAN ARMY ORDER, No. 64, OF 1904.)

Officers under the 1886 Leave Rules or the 1875 Furlough Rules may be permitted to combine any privilege leave due to them with leave or private affairs, provided that the period of such combined leave does not exceed eight months. Any extension beyond this period

will involve the conversion of the whole period into ordinary leave. Combined leave must be taken between 15th Feb. and 15th Nov., except in the case of officers of the Indian Medical Service, the Military Works Services, and the Military Accounts Department.

FURLOUGH AND LEAVE RULES OF 1875.

(WITH SUBSEQUENT ALTERATIONS.)

G.G.O. No. 1316, dated 30th December, 1875.

I. Officers included in Schedule A may receive, after five years' service in India or under the Government of India, from the date of coming under these rules, one year's furlough, and an additional year for each subsequent five years' service. Furlough not taken at the time it becomes due is not lost, and can, subject to these rules, be taken later.

NOTE.—Service for furlough under these rules, in the case of an officer electing them, is reckoned from the date of his election. (See also Notes 2, 3, and 4 to Schedule A.)

II. An officer may receive at any time any portion of the furlough to which he is entitled; but, except under Rule VIII, he may not be absent from duty on any ground for more than three years consecutively; nor, on return from furlough, may he receive, except under Rule VI, any further portion of the furlough to which he is entitled until after the interval of two years.

III. It must be distinctly understood that furlough is subject to the exigencies of the public service. Officers commanding regiments, and Heads of Departments, will forward no application for furlough unless able to satisfy the superior authorities that the officer's services can be spared without any inconvenience to the public service.

IV. Furlough does not involve forfeiture of appointment, may be spent anywhere, and counts as service for pension. (See Rule VIII.)

Note.—All furlough necessitated by wounds or illness contracted by active service in the field is treated as "extraordinary furlough" to the extent of two years, reckoning as service for pension, and as not subject to repayment, irrespective of whether it is earned by length of service or not.

V. A commissioned officer on furlough receives (up to, but not beyond, the sum of £1,000 a year) half the emoluments of his office, computed at 1s. 6d. to the rupee, the privileged rate of exchange at which furlough allowances are payable from the Home Treasury; but in any case he does not receive less than £250 a year. The emoluments of his office include all pay, staff salary, and Indian allowances he would receive in respect to it if he were on duty, and for the purpose of each payment his office is the office he fills on the day when that payment becomes due. (See Note 2 to Rule VI and Rule VII.)

2. A Departmental Officer with honorary rank or Warrant Officer, a Senior Assistant Surgeon or Assistant Surgeon, receives half the emoluments of his office, calculated in the same manner as those of Commissioned Officers, no such Officer, however, receiving as furlough pay less than the English furlough pay of his rank.

VI. Upon certificate of a Medical Board, an officer not entitled to furlough, if he has served not less than two years in India, may receive it for any period not exceeding two years; but such furlough will be deducted from his next accruing furlough until it is paid off. An officer will not be allowed to anticipate furlough under this rule to an aggregate amount of more than two years.

NOTE (1).—The grant of furlough on medical certificate is restricted to a maximum period of one year in the first instance.

NOTE (2).—An officer of less than two years' service from date of coming under the new rules, falling sick, will be sent to England on medical certificate, on English furlough pay, without the period of his furlough being fixed.

INDIAN ARMY—continued.FURLOUGH RULES OF 1875—continued.

subject to the decision of His Majesty's Government as to his ultimate disposal; a special report on each case being submitted to His Majesty's Government by the Government of India. (See Notes 2 and 3 to Schedule A.)

NOTE (3).—Furlough to the extent of two years in all may be anticipated a second time under Rule VI when the first anticipation was rendered necessary by wounds or illness contracted by active service in the field.

VII. An officer who has exhausted the furlough obtainable under these rules, and who may be certified by a Medical Board to be still unfit for duty, will be placed temporarily or permanently on half pay, according to the circumstances of the case.

VIII. Under very urgent circumstances, special furlough may be granted for private affairs, which need not be paid back; but it will be in all cases without any kind of pay, and will not count for pension.

NOTE.—The amount of leave which may be granted under this rule will be restricted to such period as the Government granting it is satisfied is actually necessary with reference to the urgency of the case.

IX. Officers proceeding on furlough to Europe may, if they desire it, receive an advance on the scale shown on page 339.

X. Leave may be granted for any period not exceeding six months at a time, and may not exceed for very urgent reasons, to be specially reported to the Government of India, be renewed till after six months have elapsed from its expiration. It must be spent at some place east of the 40th degree of east longitude. Subject to these conditions, it may be granted or refused entirely at the discretion of the local authorities, who in granting it shall fix its duration, and may fix the limits within which it shall be spent, and whether during its continuance half pay and allowances are to be deducted for the payment of a substitute; and they may grant it as preparatory or subsidiary to furlough, or for the purpose of a medical examination. Leave counts as service for pension and furlough.

The local authorities shall be defined for the purpose of this rule by the Government of India.

XI. Leave and furlough are reckoned from and to the close of the days named in the order granting them.

NOTE.—From and after the 17th November, 1883, the day on which the vessel on which an officer proceeds on furlough quits her moorings or anchorage, whether she leaves the limits of the port or not on that day, will be reckoned as the date of the commencement of his furlough, and as a day of the furlough; and the date on which the vessel on which he returns to India drops her anchor or is moored at any Indian port, will be reckoned as a day of duty. An officer's furlough begins on the vessel's departure from any Indian port at which he first joins her, and ends on the day before her arrival at the port at which he last quits her.

XII. The position of the officers not included in Schedule A will be unchanged as respects leave or furlough.

XIII. Officers now in the service placing themselves, from a given date, under these rules, shall count service for pension up to that date under the rules to which they were then subject.

NOTE.—An officer who elects the regulations of 1875, takes with him so much service for pension as he had become entitled to under former rules.

When, however, officers with less than 20 years' service under the rules of 1854 or 1868, come under these rules, they shall, after completing 20 years' service in all, be permitted to count as service for pension a proportion of any furlough or leave they may have taken under earlier rules, at the rate of two years' leave for 20 years' service, or one-tenth. Thus, an officer who, before coming under the rules of 1875, had a service of 14 years and 99 days, of which two years 142 days were on leave under the rules of 1858, would count one year and 156 days' leave, and would have 13 years 113 days' total service for pension.

SCHEDULE A.

Officers entering the Indian Army or Indian Medical Service, and Warrant Officers attaining that rank, after the 31st December, 1875; and

Any other officers, Commissioned, Departmental with honorary rank, or Warrant, belonging to the Indian Army or Medical Service, who, after the 31st December, 1875, shall, before asking for leave or furlough, apply in writing to the Government of India to be placed definitely upon this Schedule; but their service for furlough under Rule I shall commence from the date of such entry or such application respectively.

NOTE 1.—The date of entry into the late Staff Corps is that for choice of furlough rules, viz., the date on which the officer was originally appointed on probation. An officer, therefore, whose date of admission to the Staff Corps is not after the 31st December, 1875, will have

the choice of rules, but an officer whose date of admission is after that date will have no option.

NOTE 2.—Officers compulsorily placed under these regulations on entering the Staff Corps, will be allowed to count their previous military service in India towards furlough, provided they shall have served five years in India, or which not less than half shall have been in the Indian Army; but any leave out of India on private affairs taken by them during service so counted will be reckoned as furlough taken under Rule I, and any leave taken on Medical certificate will be reckoned as taken under Rule VI.

Should, therefore, an officer, who took sick leave out of India whilst in the British service, again require leave on Medical certificate on his admission to the Staff Corps, and proceed under Rule VI, he will be granted, in the first instance, only so much furlough as will com-

INDIAN ARMY—continued.FURLOUGH RULES OF 1875—continued.

plete the period admissible under that rule. This furlough may be extended to two years on "English Furlough Pay," but it will rest with His Majesty's Government to determine whether any, and if so, what, extension shall be granted.

NOTE 3.—Departmental Officers with honorary rank or Warrant Officers promoted to warrant grade after the 31st December, 1875, and thus compulsorily brought under these rules, will be allowed to count their previous service for furlough under Rule VI; but furlough is not to be granted under this rule until a Warrant Officer has completed two years' service since his last return to duty from furlough as a Non-Commissioned Officer, otherwise he can only be granted leave under Note 2 to Rule VI.

NOTE 4.—An officer electing these regulations and having furlough to his credit under previous rules, forfeits such furlough absolutely, he being required to serve five years from date of such election to entitle him to one years' furlough,

unless it be sick furlough. An officer similarly circumstanced, but having furlough to his debit, is equally with the others entitled to take one year's furlough five years after electing the new rules.

Any officers in civil employment declared by the Government of India to be under Civil Leave rules shall not be under these rules.

Note.—These regulations are not applicable to Commissioned Officers employed in the Public Works Department, but they are applicable to Departmental Officers with honorary rank and Warrant Officers so employed.

** The last pay certificate issued to an officer to whom furlough or leave has been granted shall state the pay to which, under Rule V, he is at that date entitled in respect to the office he is holding, noticing the date, if any fixed, at which he will vacate it.

SUBSIDIARY RULES.

I. No "extra or special allowances" drawn by an officer, which are now excluded from the account in calculating furlough pay under the rules of 1868, can be reckoned in calculating furlough pay under these rules (Rule V).

II. The Government of India, or His Excellency the Commander-in-Chief in India, or Lieutenant-Generals Commanding the Forces, in regard to officers serving directly under them, shall be held to be the "local authorities" named in Rule X.

III. Pending further orders, a Staff Officer granted leave under Rule X shall receive, as heretofore, the pay of his rank, and half the staff salary of his appointment during such leave; but full staff salary may be allowed him for the first sixty days (as now during Privilege Leave) when he makes approved arrangements for the performance of his duties during that period. In such cases the G. O. granting the leave must state that the first sixty days are to be on full staff pay.

The leave up to sixty days in each year on full staff pay under the foregoing rule may be taken either for the full time or by instalments, in the same way as Privilege Leave under other furlough rules is taken, the condition that six months must elapse between the return of an officer to duty and the grant of a fresh period of leave not being enforced in respect of such leave of absence.

NOTE 1.—Privilege Leave for 90 days, after 33 months' duty, which may be spent anywhere in or out of India, is admissible to Commissioned and Warrant Officers under these rules; but to claim the privilege it shall be necessary for an officer to show that he has had no leave on full Indian pay and allowances for 33 months previous to the date of his application, and that he has made satisfactory arrangements for the performance of his duties during his absence, at no additional cost to the State. If leave for a shorter period than 90 days is taken, the balance is forfeited.

NOTE 2.—Officers are not required to report themselves to the India Office should they go to England; but it will be incumbent on them to keep their departmental superiors in India acquainted with their address.

NOTE 3.—No furlough or leave out of India, if exceeding six months, can be counted as part of the required 33 months' duty; but an officer who may have 33 months' service at credit before proceeding on such furlough or leave (in excess of six months) may be allowed to take accumulated Privilege Leave on his return, if he can be spared.

NOTE 4.—Warrant Officers proceeding out of India on Privilege Leave under Note 1 must deposit the amount of their return passage money, or give security for the payment of the same.

NOTE 5.—General Officers Commanding Divisions, Districts, and Brigades, are not eligible for the 90 days' Privilege Leave referred to in the above Note 1.

NOTE 6.—The "Staff Officers" referred to in the above Rule III apply to regimental as well as Departmental Staff.

N.B.—It is distinctly to be understood that the rule regarding the pay to be drawn on leave under Rule X is liable to be modified at any time in any way that the Government of India may deem expedient.

SUPPLEMENTARY REGULATIONS FOR MILITARY OFFICERS IN CIVIL EMPLOY:—

I. Any officer now in civil employ, who is subject to the Military Furlough Regulations of 1854 or 1868, shall remain so subject.

II. Any Military Officer who may hereafter enter civil employ, who is not already subject to the Military Furlough and Leave Rules of 1875, shall remain subject to the Rules applicable to him when he enters civil employ.

NOTE.—An officer referred to in Rules I and II cannot elect the Military Furlough and Leave

INDIAN ARMY—continued.FURLOUGH RULES OF 1875—continued.

Rules of 1875, so long as he continues in civil employ.

III. Any officer who may enter civil employ, who is already subject to the Military Furlough and Leave Rules of 1875, shall be subject to the rules in the Civil Leave Code applicable to covenanted civil servants, both as regards furlough and leave taken out of India and in India.

IV. If an officer, who has been subject to the rules in the Civil Service Regulations under the third of these rules, is again employed in the Military Department, and so again becomes sub-

ject to the Military Furlough and Leave Rules of 1875, any furlough or special leave taken by him under the Civil Service Regulations will be treated in calculating the furlough admissible to him under the said Military Rules as if it had been furlough taken under the said Military Rules. Privilege Leave taken under the Civil Service Regulations will be treated as if it had been leave taken under the 10th Military Rule. Subsidiary or Examination Leave taken under the Civil Service Regulations will not affect the claims of such an officer under the Military Furlough and Leave Rules.

Temporary Half Pay.

An officer placed on temporary half pay at the recommendation of the Medical Board of the India Office will be examined by the same Board as to his fitness to return to duty every two years, and if still reported unfit at the third examination, that is, after being six years on the temporary half-pay list, he will be transferred to the retired list on permanent half pay, or on pension if entitled thereto. An officer reported to be unfit at the commencement of the second period, i.e., after two years on half pay, may, however, be permitted voluntarily to retire on permanent half pay or pension.

An officer unable to appear before the Medical Board at the stated times will be required to furnish a certificate, signed by two medical men, as to his inability. An officer who is continuously unable to appear will be retired permanently after six years without further report.

Officers who, in view to being restored to the active list, may wish to appear before the Board

at other times than those specified, may be permitted to do so. Officers ordered periodically to appear before the Board will receive their travelling expenses and lodging allowance on the War Office scale, but those appearing at other times, at their own wish, must do so at their own expense.

If the removal to half pay was in consequence of ill-health contracted in the performance of military duty, time on temporary half pay not exceeding one year is allowed to reckon as service for pension, and, after restoration to full pay, for promotion also; but an officer cannot be promoted while on the half-pay list. Transfer of an officer to temporary half pay involves the vacation of his regimental or other appointment.

An officer on temporary half pay must not proceed beyond the limits of the United Kingdom without the special permission of the India Office.

India Office.
April, 1901.

Retirement.

Officers* are subject to compulsory retirement on attaining the following ages:—

Yearly.
Lieutenant-Colonels ... .. 55
Colonels ... .. 57
Major-Generals ... .. 62
Lieutenant-Generals ... .. 67
Generals ... .. 67

A Colonel, who on attaining the age for transfer may hold the temporary rank of Major-General, is retained on the effective list until he ceases to hold such rank or attains the age of 62 years, whichever may first happen.

General Officers are also subject to compulsory retirement after being unemployed continuously for three years.

General Officers on retirement will receive pensions at the following rates:—

Yearly.
Major-Generals ... .. 800
Lieutenant-Generals ... .. 900
Generals ... .. 1,000

Other officers are entitled to pensions at the following rates:—

Yearly.
After 20 years' service for pension ... 250
" 24 " " " " ... 365
" 28 " " " " ... 500
" 32 " " " " ... 700

An officer who has become incapacitated for further service in India on account of unfitness caused by duty, may, after he has been two years on the temporary half-pay list, be granted an invalid pension according to the following scale:—

Per annum.
After 15 years' pensionable service 150
" 16 " " " " " 170
" 17 " " " " " 190
" 18 " " " " " 210
" 19 " " " " " 230

Service for pension reckons from date of arrival in India with the addition (except in the case of an officer exchanging into the Indian Army) of service out of India before joining the Indian Army to an extent not exceeding two years in any case.

* For officers appointed before 1st July, 1881, see Regulations for the Transfer to the Unemployed Super-numerary List on next page.

INDIAN ARMY—continued.RETIREMENT—continued.

Time on half pay not exceeding one year is allowed to reckon as service for pension in the case of an officer placed on half pay on account of ill-health contracted in the performance of military duty.

After being six years on temporary half pay, officers unfit for restoration to the effective list are transferred to the retired list on permanent half pay unless entitled to pension. After two years on half pay, an officer unfit for duty may voluntarily retire on half pay.

Officers in civil employ struck off the effective list of the army after 10 years' absence from military duty retain their claims to pension under military rules.

A retired officer is permitted, under certain conditions, to commute a portion not exceeding one-third of his pension, which commutation may take place on retirement, or at any time subsequent thereto. (See page 340.)

Retired officers are liable to be recalled to service in time of emergency.

Regulations for the Transfer to the Unemployed Supernumerary List.

1. Officers who were appointed to the Indian Army or the Indian Staff Corps before the 1st July, 1881, are transferred to the Unemployed Supernumerary List, on attaining the following ages:—

Lieutenant-Colonels... 55 years.
Colonels ... 57 "
Major-Generals ... 62 "
Lieutenant-Generals... } 67 "
Generals ...

2. A Colonel, who on attaining the age for transfer may hold the temporary rank of Major-General, is retained on the effective list until he ceases to hold such rank or attains the age of 62 years, whichever may first happen.

3. General officers are also transferred to the

Unemployed Supernumerary List after the following periods of non-employment:—

A General or a Lieutenant-General who attained the latter rank before the 1st January, 1901, and a Major-General who was promoted to that rank before the same date, after being unemployed for five years continuously since promotion to Major-General, or after being unemployed for seven years continuously from date of last employment as Colonel.

Other General Officers after being unemployed continuously for three years.

4. In special cases the transfer of officers to the Unemployed Supernumerary List may be delayed, or they may be temporarily brought back from it. Under either of these circumstances an officer would be supernumerary on the effective list.

FAMILY PENSIONS.

The claims to pension of the widows and families of officers of the Indian Army are treated under the provisions of such Royal Warrant regulating the grant of pension to the widows and families of officers of H.M. British Forces, as may be in force for the time being.

The following are the rates of ordinary pension:—

Pension to Widow.
Yearly.		 Compassionate allowance to each child.
Yearly.
General Officer, including £ £
Brigadier-General ... 120 20
Colonel ... 100 16
Lieutenant-Colonel ... 90 16
Major ... 70 14
Captain ... 50 12
Lieutenant ... 40 10

with increased pensions in the cases of the widows and children of officers who have been killed in action or have died of wounds or illness contracted on field service.

The compassionate allowance of a son ceases at 18 years of age, and that of a daughter at 21 years of age, or at the expiration of the year

commencing on the 1st April in which the son is otherwise previously provided for or the daughter is married.

Widows and families are also entitled to pensions under the Indian Military Service Family Pension Regulations, for which all officers must subscribe as a condition of their appointment to the Indian Army.

The following are the rates of widows' pension:—

Yearly.
£
Officers of 31 years' service from date of first commission* ... 160
Officers of 26 years' service from date of first commission† ... 130
Officers of 18 years' service from date of first commission† ... 100
Officers of 9 years' service from date of first commission† ... 70
Officers under 9 years' service from date of first commission. ... 40

Children's pensions (all ranks alike) dependent on age:—

From 10l. to 45l. per annum.

Pensions to sons cease at 21 years of age, to daughters on marriage.

* If subscribed for. Subscription for this pension is voluntary.

† If subscribed for. No officer is obliged to subscribe for these pensions who has not attained the ranks of Lieutenant-Colonel, Major, or Captain respectively.
